question: Compare Cryptography and Network Security in terms of course focus and major topics.
answer: The course focus of Cryptography and Network Security is distinct, with Cryptography primarily focusing on the principles and practice of cryptography algorithms and fundamentals of security protocols and techniques, whereas Network Security covers a broader range of topics including network-based security threats and vulnerabilities. This difference in focus is evident in the course outcomes, with Cryptography aiming to enable students to understand fundamental concepts in number theory, field theory, and cryptography (CO1), analyze principles of symmetric and asymmetric encryption algorithms (CO2), apply cryptographic techniques to solve practical problems (CO3), and evaluate different cryptographic algorithms (CO4). In contrast, Network Security is not explicitly mentioned in the provided contexts, suggesting that it is not a part of the current course.

Topics Number of Sessions Course ( 48) Outcomes
Number Theory and Finite Fields Modular arithmetic, Euclidian 7 CO1 Algorithm, Primality Testing, Fermat’s and Euler’s theorem, Chinese Reminder theorem, Discrete Logarithms, Finite Fields.
Basic of Cryptography 3 CO2 Cryptography and basic terminologies, Security goals, Security Threats and Vulnerabilities, Security Services, Security Mechanisms
Classical Encryption Techniques, Symmetric Encryption- Basic 7 CO2 Theory of Enciphering, Stream Cipher, Block Encryption Algorithms: DES, AES, Block Cipher Modes of operation.
Asymmetric key cryptographic techniques: principles – RSA – 7 CO2 ElGamal - Elliptic Curve cryptography – Key distribution and Key exchange protocols
Message Authentication Code (MAC), Hash functions, Secure 7 CO3 Hash Algorithm: SHA-1, MD5, Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC).
Digital Signature 6 CO3 Digital Signature Standard, Digital Signature Algorithm, RSA Digital Signature, Elliptic Curve Digital Signature algorithm.
User Authentication Mechanisms 5 CO3 Authentication Fundamentals, Passwords, Authentication Tokens, Certificate-Based Authentication, Biometric Authentication, Kerberos
Historical Cryptanalysis, Cryptanalysis of block ciphers, Brute 6 CO4 force attack, Standard ASCII attack, Man in the middle attack, Cryptanalysis of hash functions.

Aim of the course : The goal of this course is to understand the principles and practice of cryptography algorithms and fundamentals of security protocols and techniques. It covers cryptography, network-based security threats and vulnerabilities, and practical solutions to system and security.
Course Overview and Context: Cryptography is a VI semester core course in computer science and engineering undergraduate program. This course focuses on the principles and applications of cryptographic techniques. This course will provide overview of classical encryption techniques, block ciphers and the data encryption standard, finite fields, advanced encryption standard, confidentiality using symmetric encryption, p ublic-key cryptography, key management, hash and MAC algorithms, digital signatures, and authentication applications. Course Outcomes (CO) : At the end of the course the students should be able to do the following: Course Outcome Statement Mapped Component
Associated Activities / Assessment
CO1 Understand the fundamental concepts in number theory, field theory, and cryptography.
Employability → Intensive theory sessions on number theory, modular arithmetic, and cryptographic foundations. → In-class explanations of mathematical tools used in cryptography. → Concept-based assignments and close notebook examinations.
CO2 Analyze the principles of symmetric and asymmetric encryption algorithms.
Skill Development
→ In-depth classroom discussions on symmetric and asymmetric encryption algorithms. → Algorithm walkthroughs and step-by-step encryption/decryption demonstrations. → Quiz-based assessment using MCQs to test analytical understanding.

CO3 Apply cryptographic techniques to solve practical p roblems related to data confidentiality, integrity, and authentication.sensors,
Skill Development
→ Problem-solving sessions based on encryption, hashing, and digital signature techniques. → Assignment-based application of cryptographic methods to real-world security scenarios.
actuators and cloud service p latforms.

CO4 Evaluate different cryptographic algorithms b ased on their strengths, weaknesses, and suitability for various applications.
Entrepreneurship, Skill Development
→ Comparative analysis of cryptographic algorithms for secure communication systems. → Evaluation-focused questions in end semester examination.

The final grade will be based on the marks / grades obtained in the mid-semester and end-semester evaluation along with other assessments defined below. Relative grading method defined in the academic regulations of the university will be followed to grade the students. Student has to secure minimum 40% of marks after completing all the assessments in the following table to become eligible for grading.
